questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}}=26\left[\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left(\frac{2^5}{5}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_4\right)+\left(\frac{2^7}{7}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_6\right)+\left(\frac{2^9}{9}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_8\right)+\ldots .+\left(\frac{2^{13}}{13}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{12}\right)\right]$

$=26\left[\sum\limits_{r=1}^6\left(\frac{2^{2 r+1}}{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r}\right)\right]$.

$\boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}}=\frac{26}{13}\left[\sum\limits_{r=1}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r+1}\right)\right]$

$=2\left[\sum\limits_{r=0}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r+1}\right)-\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right) \cdot 2^1\right]$

$=2\left[\sum\limits_{r=0}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} C_{2 r+1}\right)\right]-52$

$\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}} & =2\left[\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right) \cdot 2^1+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_3\right) \cdot 2^3+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_5\right) \cdot 2^5+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_7\right) \cdot 2^7+\ldots . .+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{13}\right) \cdot 2^{13}\right]-52 \\ & =(1+2)^{13}-(1-2)^{13}-52 \\ & =3^{13}+1-52 \\ & =3^{13}-51.....(i)\end{aligned}$

$$ \boldsymbol{S}_r=26\left[\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left(\frac{2^5}{5}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_4\right)+\left(\frac{2^7}{7}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_6\right)+\left(\frac{2^9}{9}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_8\right)+\ldots .+\left(\frac{2^{13}}{13}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{12}\right)\right]=3^{13}-\boldsymbol{\alpha} \ldots . (ii)$$

From the equivalence relationship between equation $(\boldsymbol{i})$ and $(\boldsymbol{i i})$ we conclude $\alpha=51$

questions.questionText: If the coefficients of the middle terms in the binomial expansions of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ and $(1-\alpha x)^{28}, \alpha \neq 0$, are equal, then the value of $\alpha$ is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For a binomial expansion $(a+b)^n$:

  • if $n$ is even, there is only one middle term,

  • and the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{n}{2}+1\right)$th term.

We are given:

$$ (1+\alpha x)^{26} \quad \text{and} \quad (1-\alpha x)^{28} $$

and the coefficients of their middle terms are equal.

1. Middle term of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$

Since $26$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{26}{2}+1\right)=14$th term.

The general term in $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{26}{r}(1)^{26-r}(\alpha x)^r $$

So the $14$th term corresponds to $r=13$:

$$ T_{14}=\binom{26}{13}(\alpha x)^{13} $$

Hence, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13} $$

2. Middle term of $(1-\alpha x)^{28}$

Since $28$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{28}{2}+1\right)=15$th term.

The general term is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{28}{r}(1)^{28-r}(-\alpha x)^r $$

So the $15$th term corresponds to $r=14$:

$$ T_{15}=\binom{28}{14}(-\alpha x)^{14} $$

Since $14$ is even,

$$ (-\alpha x)^{14}=\alpha^{14}x^{14} $$

Thus, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

3. Equate the coefficients

Given that these coefficients are equal:

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

Since $\alpha\neq 0$, divide both sides by $\alpha^{13}$:

$$ \binom{26}{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha $$

So,

$$ \alpha=\frac{\binom{26}{13}}{\binom{28}{14}} $$

4. Simplify

$$ \alpha=\frac{\dfrac{26!}{13!13!}}{\dfrac{28!}{14!14!}} $$

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!}{13!13!}\cdot \frac{14!14!}{28!} $$

Now use

$$ 14!=14\cdot 13! $$

So,

$$ 14!14!=(14\cdot 13!)(14\cdot 13!)=14^2(13!)^2 $$

Thus,

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!\cdot 14^2(13!)^2}{(13!)^2\cdot 28!} =\frac{26!\cdot 14^2}{28!} $$

Also,

$$ 28!=28\cdot 27\cdot 26! $$

Therefore,

$$ \alpha=\frac{14^2}{28\cdot 27} =\frac{14}{2\cdot 27} =\frac{7}{27} $$

$$ \boxed{\alpha=\frac{7}{27}} $$

So, the correct option is D.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The coefficient of $x^2$ in the expansion of $\left(2 x^2+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{10}, x \neq 0$, is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Using the binomial theorem,

$$ \left(2x^2+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{10} = \sum_{r=0}^{10} \binom{10}{r} (2x^2)^{10-r}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^r $$

Now simplify the general term:

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{10}{r} 2^{10-r} x^{2(10-r)} x^{-r} $$

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{10}{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-2r-r} =\binom{10}{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-3r} $$

We need the coefficient of $x^2$, so set the power of $x$ equal to $2$:

$$ 20-3r=2 $$

$$ 3r=18 $$

$$ r=6 $$

Now substitute $r=6$:

$$ \text{Required coefficient}=\binom{10}{6}2^{10-6} $$

$$ =\binom{10}{6}2^4 $$

$$ =210 \times 16 $$

$$ =3360 $$

So, the coefficient of $x^2$ is

$$ \boxed{3360} $$

Hence, the correct option is B.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If the sum of the coefficients of $x^7$ and $x^{14}$ in the expansion of $\left(\frac{1}{x^3}-x^4\right)^n, x \neq 0$, is zero, then the value of $n$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For

$$ \left(\frac{1}{x^3}-x^4\right)^n = \left(x^{-3}-x^4\right)^n, $$

let us write the general term in the expansion.

The general term is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{n}{r}(x^{-3})^{\,n-r}(-x^4)^r $$

$$ =\binom{n}{r}(-1)^r x^{-3(n-r)}x^{4r} $$

$$ =\binom{n}{r}(-1)^r x^{-3n+3r+4r} $$

$$ =\binom{n}{r}(-1)^r x^{-3n+7r}. $$

So, the coefficient of $x^k$ comes from the term for which

$$ -3n+7r=k. $$

Now find the coefficients of $x^7$ and $x^{14}$.

For $x^7$:

$$ -3n+7r=7 $$

$$ 7r=3n+7 $$

$$ r=\frac{3n+7}{7}. $$

For $x^{14}$:

$$ -3n+7r=14 $$

$$ 7r=3n+14 $$

$$ r=\frac{3n+14}{7}. $$

Since $r$ must be an integer, $3n+7$ and $3n+14$ must both be divisible by $7$.

Now,

$$ 3n+7\equiv 0 \pmod{7} \Rightarrow 3n\equiv 0 \pmod{7}. $$

Since $3$ and $7$ are coprime,

$$ n\equiv 0 \pmod{7}. $$

So let

$$ n=7m. $$

Then the two corresponding values of $r$ are

For $x^7$:

$$ r_1=\frac{3(7m)+7}{7}=3m+1. $$

For $x^{14}$:

$$ r_2=\frac{3(7m)+14}{7}=3m+2. $$

Hence the coefficients are

$$ \binom{n}{3m+1}(-1)^{3m+1} \quad \text{and} \quad \binom{n}{3m+2}(-1)^{3m+2}. $$

Their sum is zero, so

$$ \binom{n}{3m+1}(-1)^{3m+1}+\binom{n}{3m+2}(-1)^{3m+2}=0. $$

Taking common sign,

$$ -\binom{n}{3m+1}+\binom{n}{3m+2}=0 $$

or

$$ \binom{n}{3m+2}=\binom{n}{3m+1}. $$

Using the NCERT result:

$$ \binom{n}{r}=\binom{n}{r+1} \Rightarrow r+1=\frac{n}{2} $$

or equivalently,

$$ 2r+1=n. $$

Here,

$$ r=3m+1. $$

So,

$$ 2(3m+1)+1=7m $$

$$ 6m+2+1=7m $$

$$ 6m+3=7m $$

$$ m=3. $$

Therefore,

$$ n=7m=21. $$

Hence, the value of $n$ is

$$ \boxed{21}. $$

questions.questionText: In the expansion of $\left(9 x-\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{x}}\right)^{18}, x>0$, if the term independent of $x$ is (221)k, then k is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the expansion of

$$ \left(9x-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^{18}, $$

we use the general term in the binomial expansion:

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{18}{r}(9x)^{18-r}\left(-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^r $$

Now simplify the power of $x$ in this term.

Since

$$ (9x)^{18-r}=9^{18-r}x^{18-r} $$

and

$$ \left(\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^r=\frac{1}{3^r x^{r/2}}, $$

so the power of $x$ in the general term is

$$ x^{18-r-\frac r2}=x^{18-\frac{3r}{2}}. $$

For the term independent of $x$, the power of $x$ must be zero:

$$ 18-\frac{3r}{2}=0 $$

$$ \frac{3r}{2}=18 $$

$$ r=12 $$

So the required term is the $(12+1)$th term, i.e. $T_{13}$.

Now compute it:

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12}(9x)^6\left(-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^{12} $$

Since the power of $x$ becomes zero, only the numerical coefficient remains:

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12}\cdot 9^6 \cdot \frac{1}{3^{12}} $$

Also,

$$ 9^6=(3^2)^6=3^{12} $$

So,

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12} $$

and

$$ \binom{18}{12}=\binom{18}{6}=\frac{18!}{6!\,12!}=18564 $$

Given that the term independent of $x$ is $(221)k$, we have

$$ 221k=18564 $$

Thus,

$$ k=\frac{18564}{221}=84 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{84} $$

Hence, Option A is correct.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let the smallest value of $k \in \mathbb{N}$, for which the coefficient of $x^3$ in $(1+x)^3+(1+x)^4+(1+x)^5+\ldots+(1+x)^{99}+(1+k x)^{100}, x \neq 0$, is $\left(43 n+\frac{101}{4}\right)\left({ }^{100} \mathrm{C}_3\right)$ for some $n \in \mathrm{~N}$, be $p$. Then the value of $p+n$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Coefficient of $x^3$ in the given expression

$$ (1+x)^3+(1+x)^4+\cdots +(1+x)^{99}+(1+kx)^{100} $$

is obtained by adding the coefficients of $x^3$ from each term.

For $(1+x)^r$, coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ {}^rC_3 $$

So, from

$$ (1+x)^3+(1+x)^4+\cdots +(1+x)^{99} $$

the total coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ \sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^rC_3 $$

Also, in $(1+kx)^{100}$, coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ {}^{100}C_3\,k^3 $$

Hence total coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$ \sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^rC_3 + {}^{100}C_3 k^3 $$

Now use the standard NCERT identity:

$$ \sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^rC_3 = {}^{100}C_4 $$

So coefficient becomes

$$ {}^{100}C_4 + {}^{100}C_3 k^3 $$

Given that this equals

$$ \left(43n+\frac{101}{4}\right){}^{100}C_3 $$

Therefore,

$$ {}^{100}C_4 + {}^{100}C_3 k^3 = \left(43n+\frac{101}{4}\right){}^{100}C_3 $$

Divide by ${}^{100}C_3$:

$$ \frac{{}^{100}C_4}{{}^{100}C_3}+k^3 = 43n+\frac{101}{4} $$

Now,

$$ \frac{{}^{100}C_4}{{}^{100}C_3} = \frac{100-3}{4} = \frac{97}{4} $$

So,

$$ k^3+\frac{97}{4}=43n+\frac{101}{4} $$

Thus,

$$ k^3=43n+1 $$

We need the smallest $k\in\mathbb N$ such that

$$ k^3-1 $$

is divisible by $43$.

That is,

$$ k^3\equiv 1 \pmod{43} $$

Now check smallest natural numbers:

$$ 1^3=1 \equiv 1 \pmod{43} $$

So the smallest value is

$$ k=1 $$

Hence,

$$ 1=43n+1 $$

which gives

$$ n=0 $$

But in the question, $n\in \mathbb N$. In school-level convention, if $\mathbb N=\{0,1,2,\dots\}$, then this is allowed.

Thus,

$$ p=1,\quad n=0 $$

and

$$ p+n=1 $$

But this is not among the options, so clearly the intended convention is $n\ge 1$.

Now take the smallest $n\in\mathbb N$ with $n\ge 1$ such that

$$ k^3=43n+1 $$

Try $n=1$:

$$ k^3=44 \quad \text{not a cube} $$

$n=2$:

$$ k^3=87 \quad \text{not a cube} $$

$n=3$:

$$ k^3=130 \quad \text{not a cube} $$

$n=4$:

$$ k^3=173 $$

$n=5$:

$$ k^3=216=6^3 $$

So smallest such $k$ is

$$ p=6,\quad n=5 $$

Therefore,

$$ p+n=6+5=11 $$

Hence the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{11} $$

So, Option B is correct.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I :

$25^{13} + 20^{13} + 8^{13} + 3^{13}$ is divisible by 7.

Statement II :

The integral part of $(7 + 4\sqrt{3})^{25}$ is an odd number.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Statement I : $25^{13}+20^{13}+8^{13}+3^{13}$ is divisible by 7.

Key concept : If $n$ is odd, $x^n+y^n=(x+y)\left(x^{n-1}-x^{n-2} y+x^{n-3} y^2-\cdots-x y^{n-2}+\right. y^{n-1}$ ) So, $x^n+y^n$ is divisible by $x+y$.

In the given statement :

  • $25^{13}+3^{13}$ is divisible by $25+3=28$.

  • $25^{13}+3^{13}$ is also divisible by 7 because 28 is divisible by 7 .

  • So, $25^{13}+3^{13}=7 \cdot I_1$.........(1) ( $I_1$ is a positive integer).

Similarly :

  • $20^{13}+8^{13}$ is divisible by $20+8=28$.

  • $20^{13}+8^{13}$ is divisible by 7.

  • So, $20^{13}+8^{13}=7 \cdot I_2$ ..........(2) ( $I_2$ is a positive integer).

Add (1) & (2) :

  • $25^{13}+3^{13}+20^{13}+8^{13}=7\left(I_1+I_2\right)$

So, $25^{13}+20^{13}+8^{13}+3^{13}$ is divisible by 7 . Statement I is true.

Statement : The integral part of $(7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}$ is an odd number.

Let $X=(7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}$. Let $I$ be the integral part and $f$ be the fractional part ( $0

Adding the two expansions:

$$ (7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}+(7-4 \sqrt{3})^{25}=I+f+f^{\prime} $$

Using the binomial expansion $(a+b)^n+(a-b)^n=2\left[a^n+\binom{n}{2} a^{n-2} b^2+\ldots\right]$, we see that the sum is always an even integer because of the factor of 2 .

$$ I+f+f^{\prime}=\text { Even Integer } $$

Since $I$ is an integer, $f+f^{\prime}$ must also be an integer. Given $0 < f < 1$ and $0 < f^{\prime} < 1$, the only possible integer value for their sum is $f+f^{\prime}=1$.

$$ I+1=\text { Even Integer } $$

$$ I=\text { Even Integer }-1=\text { Odd Integer } $$

The integral part I is indeed an odd number. Statement II is True.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The sum of the coefficients of $x^{499}$ and $x^{500}$ in $(1 + x)^{1000} + x(1 + x)^{999} + x^2(1 + x)^{998} + \ldots + x^{1000}$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000}+x(1+x)^{999}+x^2(1+x)^{998}+\cdots+x^{1000} $$

Notice that this is a Geometric Progression (GP) with:

  • First term (a): $(1+x)^{1000}$

  • Common ratio $(r): \frac{x}{1+x}$

  • Number of terms ( $n$ ): 1001

The sum of a GP is given by $S=a \frac{\left(1-r^n\right)}{(1-r)}$. Substituting our values:

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000}\left[\frac{1-\left(\frac{x}{1+x}\right)^{1001}}{1-\frac{x}{1+x}}\right] $$

Simplify the denominator: $1-\frac{x}{1+x}=\frac{1+x-x}{1+x}=\frac{1}{1+x}$.

$$ S=(1+x)^{1000} \cdot(1+x)\left[1-\frac{x^{1001}}{(1+x)^{1001}}\right] $$

$$ S=(1+x)^{1001}-x^{1001} $$

Now we need the coefficients of $x^{499}$ and $x^{500}$ in the simplified expression $(1+x)^{1001}- x^{1001}$.

Since $x^{1001}$ only affects the term with power 1001, we only look at $(1+x)^{1001}$.

In the binomial expansion $(1+x)^n$

Coefficient of $x^r$ is ${ }^n C_r$

Coefficient of $x^{499}$ in $S$ :

$$ { }^{1001} C_{499} $$

Coefficient of $x^{500}$ in $S$ :

${ }^{1001} C_{500}$ (The term $x^{1001}$ does not contribute.)

$$ \text { Sum of coefficients } $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} { }^{1001} C_{499}+{ }^{1001} C_{500}={ }^{1002} C_{500} \end{array} $$

[$\text { Using pascal's identity } $

${ }^n C_r+{ }^n C_{r+1}={ }^{n+1} C_{r+1}$]
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{25!}+\frac{1}{3!23!}+\frac{1}{5!21!}+\ldots$ up to 13 terms. If $13 \mathrm{~S}=\frac{2^k}{n!}, k \in \mathrm{~N}$, then $n+k$ is equal to
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ S=\frac{1}{1!25!}+\frac{1}{3!23!}+\frac{1}{5!21!}+\ldots 13 \text { terms } $$

multiply in numerator and denominator by 26 !

$$ \begin{aligned} & S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[\frac{26!}{1!25!}+\frac{26!}{3!23!}+\frac{26!}{5!2!}+\ldots 13 \text { term }\right] \\ & S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[{ }^{26} C_1+{ }^{26} C_3+{ }^{26} C_5+\ldots 13 \text { term }\right],\left\{{ }^n C_r=\frac{n!}{(n-r)!r!}\right\} \end{aligned} $$

Lets find 13th term lower subscript are in A.P with, common difference $=2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & T_{13}=1+(13-1) 2=25 \\ & \text { so, } S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[{ }^{26} C_1+{ }^{26} C_3+{ }^{26} C_5+\ldots{ }^{26} C_{25}\right] \\ & S=\frac{1}{26!}\left[2^{26-1}\right],\left\{\text { because }{ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_3+{ }^n C_5 \cdots=2^{n-1}\right\} \end{aligned} $$

$$ S=\frac{2^{25}}{26!} $$

It is given that $13 S=\frac{2^K}{n!}$

substitute value of $S$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 13 \times \frac{2^{25}}{26!}=\frac{2^K}{n!} \\ & \frac{13 \times 2^{25}}{26 \times 25!}=\frac{2^K}{n!} \\ & \frac{2^{24}}{25!}=\frac{2^K}{n!} \end{aligned} $$

so $n=25, K=24$

$n+K=25+24=49$

questions.questionText: The sum of all possible values of $\mathbf{n} \in \mathbf{N}$, so that the coefficients of $x, x^2$ and $x^3$ in the expansion of $\left(1+x^2\right)^2(1+x)^{\mathrm{n}}$, are in arithmetic progression is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \left(1+x^2\right)^2\left(1+x^n\right) \\ & \left(1+x^4+2 x^2\right)(1+x)^n \\ & \text { Coeff of } x={ }^n C_1 \\ & \text { Coeff of } x^2={ }^n C_2+2 \cdot{ }^n C_0 \\ & \text { Coeff of } x^3={ }^n C_3+2 \cdot{ }^n C_1 \\ & \because \text { Coeff of } x, x^2, x^3 \rightarrow \mathrm{AP} \\ & \Rightarrow 2\left({ }^n C_2+2\right)={ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_3+2 n \\ & 2^n C_2+4={ }^n C_3+3 n \\ & \Rightarrow n^2-9 n^2+26 n-24=0 \\ & \Rightarrow n=2,3,4 \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Sum of all value of } n \text { is } 9 \end{aligned} $$

Let $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$ denote the coefficient of $x^{\mathrm{r}}$ in the binomial expansion of $(1+x)^{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}, 0 \leq \mathrm{r} \leq \mathrm{n}$. If

$P_n=C_0-C_1+\frac{2^2}{3} C_2-\frac{2^3}{4} C_3+\ldots . .+\frac{(-2)^n}{n+1} C_n$, then the value of $\sum\limits_{n=1}^{25} \frac{1}{P_{2 n}}$ equals.
